coredata.dc:title: Casein carbon dots decorated multi-walled carbon nanotubes double matrix supported potassium intercalated manganese oxide for high performance aqueous asymmetric pseudo-super capacitor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shahab, Muhammad
Ahmad, Israr
Nazeef, Noreen
Raziq, Fazal
Ali, Saharfat
Khan, Behzad
Khan, Majid
Rahman, Anis Ur
Zaman, Khair
Ibrahim, Mohammad
Muhammad, Iltaf
Ullah, Ihsan
Ikram, Muhammad
Ali, Asad
coredata.dc:description: According to Pmax = V2/(4*ESR), power density (Pmax) is proportional to the applied potential (V2) and equivalent series resistance (ESR). To overcome the limitation of narrow potential window herein, we employed in-situ hydrothermal synthesis of K-δMnO2 coupled with casein carbon dots (CCDs) decorated Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) which resulted in enhanced ion/charge conductivity, power density, energy density, porosity, specific surface area (666.387 m2/g), broaden potential window of 0–1.4 V and highly reversible redox pseudo-capacitance of 396 F/g, due to synergistic effect of elevated electrical conductivity of CCDs decorated nanotubes. At a potential window of 1.4 V fast faradic redox reactions and K+ intercalation/de-intercalation occur at the electrode-electrolyte interface in MnO2 which constrain water disintegration in kinetics. A high efficacy aqueous asymmetric super capacitor (K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs//AC) was configured with K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs as anode which manifested steady working potential window of 0–2.4 V in 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. The as configured asymmetric super capacitor has illustrated an outstanding energy density of 82 Wh/kg at a power density of 595 W/kg and specific capacitance of 103 F/g and a prolonged cycle execution with capacitance retention of 98 % over 10,000 cycles at the current density of 0.5 A/g. This approach will open new paradigms for new emerging high feasible wide potential window energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Casein carbon dots (CCDs)
Asymmetric super capacitor
Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)
Power density
Energy density

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD analysis of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs(b) FT-IR Spectra of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs (c) Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) plot of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs (d) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (e) DOS, TDOS of K-δMnO2 (f) band structure of K-δMnO2.
a) XPS Survey scans of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs b) XPS spectra of Mn 2p c) XPS spectra of O 1s d) XPS spectra of K 2p e) XPS spectra of C 1s f).

Electrochemical properties of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs a) CV of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs electrode at different potential windows and current rates Na2SO4(1M) b) CV of K-MnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs (5 %), Na2SO4(2M) c) CV of K-MnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs (10 %), Na2SO4(2M) d) CV of K-⸹MnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs (15 %), Na2SO4(2M) e) K-⸹MnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs(excess) f) GCD at various current densities g) capacitance retention vs cycle number h) plot of specific capacitance vs current density i) diffusion controlled and surface capacitive contribution to the total stored charge K-⸹MnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs.

Electrochemical properties of K-δMno2/CCDs-MWCNTs//AC a) CV of whole device at various scan rates b) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of whole device of whole device c) specific retention as a function of current density d) cycle stability over 10,000 cycles e) GCD of whole device at different current densities 1–5 A/g.

head.ce:title: Casein carbon dots decorated multi-walled carbon nanotubes double matrix supported potassium intercalated manganese oxide for high performance aqueous asymmetric pseudo-super capacitor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: According to Pmax = V2/(4*ESR), power density (Pmax) is proportional to the applied potential (V2) and equivalent series resistance (ESR). To overcome the limitation of narrow potential window herein, we employed in-situ hydrothermal synthesis of K-δMnO2 coupled with casein carbon dots (CCDs) decorated Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) which resulted in enhanced ion/charge conductivity, power density, energy density, porosity, specific surface area (666.387 m2/g), broaden potential window of 0–1.4 V and highly reversible redox pseudo-capacitance of 396 F/g, due to synergistic effect of elevated electrical conductivity of CCDs decorated nanotubes. At a potential window of 1.4 V fast faradic redox reactions and K+ intercalation/de-intercalation occur at the electrode-electrolyte interface in MnO2 which constrain water disintegration in kinetics. A high efficacy aqueous asymmetric super capacitor (K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs//AC) was configured with K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs as anode which manifested steady working potential window of 0–2.4 V in 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. The as configured asymmetric super capacitor has illustrated an outstanding energy density of 82 Wh/kg at a power density of 595 W/kg and specific capacitance of 103 F/g and a prolonged cycle execution with capacitance retention of 98 % over 10,000 cycles at the current density of 0.5 A/g. This approach will open new paradigms for new emerging high feasible wide potential window energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent times, high power and energy density materials have become the most trending topic for researchers due to the rapid increasing demand of energy accumulators in energy sectors [1]. Batteries and super capacitors are the most dependable and optimistic rapid energy accumulator amidst the different types of electrochemical energy storage devices [2]. Super capacitors are the electrochemical capacitors which are notable for elevated power density, better cycle ability but low energy density [3]. Super capacitors are becoming ubiquitous in hybrid automotive applications and as a supplementary source for vehicles operated on batteries. A large number of transition metal oxides like Fe2O3 [4,5], ZnO [6,7], Co3O4 [8,9], NiO [10,11], RuO2 [12,13], XCo2O4 (X = Mn, Cu, Ni) [14], MnO2 [15,16]. MnO2/CQDs/Graphene, (GQDs)@MnO2 [17–23], etc. have gained significant attentions as an electrode material in asymmetric super capacitors(ASCs) due to numerous oxidation states, extraordinary electrochemical stability, sample handling and feasible fabrication [4,23,24]. Among the aforementioned transition metal oxides, manganese dioxide owing to its low cost, impressive capacitive performance, non-toxicity and environmental compatibility has grabbed tremendous attention of researchers in the last few decades [25].

A presumptive capacitance of 1233 F/g is anticipated to be achieved at potential window of 0.9 V for the complete reduction of Mn+4 into Mn+3, which is much higher value but the practical capacitance is lower than the theoretical value which is usually less than 100 F/g because of the inferior ionic/charge transport and poor mechanical and thermal stability of MnO2 [26–28]. According to eq. E = 1/2C∗V2 the two well-known methods which are employed to intensification the energy density are; To enhance the capacitance of the materials and to extend the working potential window [29,30] because of its relationship with energy density according to the above equation [31,32]. The doping of metal ions in MnO2 also leads to the enhancement of its electrochemical properties [33,34]. Among metal ions, alkali ions (K+, Na+ and Li+) are found to be a very efficient method due to their extremely low ionization energy and hence provide surplus electronic system [35–37]. Among the alkali ions K+ is more efficient because of several reasons, such as robust charge mobility, low price, high redox potential, low current density and better ionic conductivity. It's extremely excellent ionic mobility is due to its smaller ionic size (0.133 nm) with the electrode material out-turned in enhanced electrochemical performance [38]. K+ have relatively low current densities and excellent ionic mobility compared to other alkali metals like Li and Na [39]. Intercalation of K+ into MnO2 produce Mn+3 which can be oxidize further during charging, indicating that Mn+3 is the key for the production of pseudo-capacitance and its amount directly affects the pseudo capacitance value. This clearly indicate that K+ has a crucial role in enhancing the electrochemical performance [40].

Having this opinion in mind, we fabricated (Scheme 1 ) K doped δMnO2 which is then coupled with Casein Carbon dots (CCDs) decorated multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) in order to overcome the defect of poor structural stability and inferior conductivity as the MWCNTs has an articulated tubular network with unique pore structure, high surface area, improved mechanical and thermal stability. CCDs have large specific surface area, outstanding electron donor/acceptor properties and charge/ion conductivity [41]. The synergistic effect of both MWCNTs and CCDs endows K-δMnO2 with enhanced specific capacitance, energy density and expandable practicable 0–1.4 V potential window and exceptional 396 F/g specific capacitance. In addition we also designed an aqueous asymmetric super capacitor K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs//AC having potential window 0–2.4 V with K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs as an anode and active carbon as a cathode which showed relatively higher energy density of 82 Wh/kg and power density of 595 W/kg. The MWCNTs and CCDs somehow minimize the current leakage from the nano-channels formed in nano-cracks of the K-δMnO2 due to avalanche of electrons coming from the system, by providing mechanical strength to the K-δMnO2, therefore leading to prolong potential window and enhanced power and energy densities.

Pseudo-capacitive materials like MnO2 involve robust and reversible Faradic redox reactions accountable for the storage of charge. In contrast to ionic adsorption in EDLC the Faradic contribution substantially enhances the ensuing capacitance and energy density of the device. In pseudo-capacitive materials the electrostatic contribution of EDLC is normally low due to restricted electronic conductivity and optimum surface area [42]. In an effort to enhance the electronic conductivity/ionic mobility and extended potential window herein, we have coupled K-⸹MnO2 with MWCNTs decorated with CCDs. The mechanistic insight of the effect of K+ in δMnO2 coupled with CCDs decorated MWCNTs was described in graphical abstract. XRD analysis was carried out as be seen in Fig. 1(a). K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs have the crystal phase δ-MnO2 (JCPDS 80-1098) [43]. The broad peak at 19.1 (2Ɵ) corresponds to hkl plane 002 (JCPDS card No. 26-1076) is attributed to carbon dots [44]. This indicate the inter layer spacing of Carbon dots (0.30 nm) higher than that of Graphene carbon dots which is confirmation of poor nature of carbon dots crystallinity. The broad peak at 2Ө at 20–30 shows MWCNTs The diffraction peaks at 12 and 37 with allocated planes (001) and (111) with interlayer spacing of 0.7 Å, are attributed to δMnO2 crystal phases of the 2D layered birnessite-type MnO2, which was sorted to be a monoclinic type with space group C2/m and unit cell parameters of a = 5.150Å, b = 2.844Å, c = 7.159Å and β = 100.64° (JCPDS 42-131711).

FT-IR spectroscopy was carried out within the range of 400–4000 cm−1 for the prediction of functional groups in the as-prepared sample. Fig. 1(b) exhibits FT-IR spectrum of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs at various coupling levels. The band at approximately 3317 cm−1 shows OH stretching vibration. The presence of OH group is responsible for hydrophobicity of CCDs. The bands at 1744 cm−1, 1635 cm−1 and 1106 cm−1 corresponds to CO, CC and CO respectively. The peak at 1377 cm−1 is associated with stretching vibration of Mn-O-Mn. Finally the characteristic peak at 718 cm−1 is associated with M-O (M = Mn, K) [45,46].

Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) analysis were performed in order to explore the porosity and surface area of the sample as presented in Fig. 1(c). The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs closely resembles the type-II isotherm as mentioned in IUPAC, with a clear distinguishable hysteresis loop which is the characteristic of mesoporous material. The presence of mesopores can effectively reduce the ion transport resistance and improved the wettability of the material [47]. Additionally, it also resulted in an increased specific surface area of the sample which was calculated to be 666.387 m2/g using BET method. Pore volume and pore size distribution of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs was determined using (Barret-Joyner-Halenda) BJH method as shown in Fig. 1(d). The net pore volume was calculated to be 0.247 cc/g and average pore width was 2.583 nm which indicates the mesoporous structure of the material having high porosity and enhanced specific surface area. The mesoporous structure provides channels for ion/charge transport and lowers the ion transport resistance, thus resulting in enhanced charge storage [48]. Fig. S1(a) shows UV–Visible spectrum of casein carbon dots. A sharp peak at 217 nm is observed which is attributed to π-π* and n-π* transition and is consistent with literature. Fig. S1(b) shows the FT-IR spectrum of pure casein carbon dots.

To thoroughly understand the valence state information of the as prepared sample K-⸹MnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed. The presence of Mn, K, C and O elements were unambiguously asserted from the survey spectrum Figs. 2(a), S5(a-d). Two significant bands at binding energies of 654 eV and 642.3 eV are seen in the spin splitting of the Mn 2p XPS spectra, and they are ascribed to the Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively Fig. 2(b). The two peaks shows a difference of 11.8 eV in spin energies which is attributed to the formation of MnO2. [46] The O 1s high-resolution spectrum is displayed in Fig. 2(c), with one major peak and one minor peaks at binding energies of 531.2 eV and 532.6 eV which correspond to CO (O lattice) and Mn-O-Mn (O surface) [48]. Two major bands having binding energies of 295.43 eV and 292.7 eV for the K 2p in K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs are correspondingly identified as the K 2p1/2 and K 2p3/2. Fig. 2(d), which attest to the sample's measurable K+ presence and prove structural stability [47]. Fig. 2(e) shows high resolution spectra of C 1s, which have one major and two minor peaks at binding energies of 292.5, 295.9 and 301.2 eV which are attributed to CC, CO and CO [49].

TEM images of the K-δMnO2/CCDs/MWCNTs are illustrated in Figs. 3 and S4 which shows clearly multi-walled carbon nanotubes decorated with CCDs. The shape of nanotubes is identical as reported in literature. TEM analysis was performed for the detailed structural study of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs. The results manifested excellent interaction between MWCNTs and CCDs and exhibit that CCDs are anchored properly on MWCNTs. It was found that MWCNTs were positioned randomly and formed an articulated network, after the incorporation of CCDs the morphology of MWCNTs is illustrated as that of Pure MWCNTs. As shown in Fig. 3(a-c), the MWCNTs are agglomerated into larger particles and are surrounded by Casein Carbon dots having 10 nm of diameter. Fig. 3(d) shows CCDs along with K-δMnO2 nanoflakes with mean diameter of 26 nm as calculated by image j software. The d spacing was calculated by digital micrograph software is 0.67 nm. These layers amplified electrochemically active sites by shortening the ion/charge mobility passageway and the diffusion path of electrolyte ions therefore enhancing the pseudo-capacitive performance of the sample.

To explore the morphology of the K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis was performed at different magnifications. Fig. S2 shows the porous structure with mean pore diameter of 2.06 nm, conferring that MWCNTs are completely covered by the K-δMnO2 forming nano-flakes. The porosity can be clearly seen in Fig. S2(b-d) which confirms the rough surface of the as prepared sample. Both the porosity and roughness are attributed to the enhance ion/charge conductivity. The porosity enhances intercalation and de-intercalation of charges/ions in the as prepared sample [49,50].Further the presence of Mn, O and K were confirmed from (Electron dispersive spectroscopy) EDS (Fig. S3).

Moreover, to have insight into the charge transportation and to align experimental results with theoretical calculations, DFT studies were carried out. The total density of states (TDOS), atom projected density of states (APDOS) projected on K, Mn and O atoms and the band structure of K-⸹MnO2 is calculated by first-principles calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) using Vienna ab-initio simulation package (VASP) and is given Fig. 1(e, f). The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdue-burke Ernzerhof (PBE) functional is used to describe the exchange and correlation and the projector augmented wave (PAW) method is considered for the interaction between atomic core and valence electrons. The cutoff energy, Gaussian smearing broadening and k-point mesh are considered 500 eV, 0.05 eV and 6 × 3 × 6, respectively. A periodic cell structure of K-δMnO2 was modeled having 8 atoms comprising of 2 K, 2 Mg and 4 oxygen atoms. Our results show that the K-δMnO2 is a magnetic semiconductor material having spin-up band gap of 1.37 eV and spin-down band gap of 2.62 eV. The d orbital of Mn, p-orbital of Oxygen and S-orbital of K interacted which resulted in crowding near Fermi level therefore reducing band gap. The decrease in band gap reduction is due to intercalation of K, which contribute in charge transportation.

To validate the electrochemical performance of the as-prepared K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs CV measurements were performed. Fig. 4(a) shows the Cyclic voltammogram for the K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs electrode in the range of 0–1.4 V at a scan rate of 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50 mV/S. At 0–0.8 V potential window the CV shows a quasi-rectangular curve with a tiny redox peak at (0.5 V), attributed to the typical CV landmark of MnO2 based electrodes [51,52]. The quasi-rectangular shape curves indicate the contribution of pseudo-capacitance in addition to electrical double-layered capacitance (EDLC). The redox peaks become more prominent at 0.4–0.5 V when the potential window is extended from 0.8 to 1.2 V. A second pair of redox peaks is seen in the region of 0.8–0.9 V when the potential window is prolonged further to 1.4 V, and these are attributed to negating the polarization of water. The appearance of first pair of redox peaks at 0.4–0.5 V is attributed to the conventional charge storage mechanism of K-δMnO2 [53], However, the reversible redox pair of Mn4+/Mn3+ with the intercalation/de-intercalation of ions/charges into the material's active sites is responsible for the second pair of redox peaks at 0.8–0.9 V [54,55]. Therefore, K-δMnO2 endows extended potential window in neutral electrolyte [56].

All the cyclic voltammetry curves at all scan rates show the quasi-rectangular shapes. It maintained its rectangular shape even though at high potential window of 1.4 V, which asserted the continuous and reversible Faradic redox reactions. To mitigate water decomposition beyond 1.2 V, the electrolytic solution was purged with argon for 10 min to ensure the removal of dissolved oxygen and the concentration of Na2SO4 was increased from 1 M to 2 M as shown in Fig. 4(b-d). However, water decomposition was still observed in K-⸹MnO2/CCDS-MWCNTs (10 %) and K-⸹MnO2/CCDS-MWCNTs (15 %) but was not observed in K-⸹MnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs (5 %) This is attributed to the hydrophilic nature of the CCDs due to the presence of hydrophilic groups like hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. Furthermore, excess of MWCNTs were added to a controlled sample, surprisingly the water decomposition diminished but the tiny redox peaks disappeared which is indication of the dominance of EDLC over pseudo-capacitive behavior as shown in Fig. 4(e).

The GCD curves of the K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs at different current densities of 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5 and 5 A/g at various working potential windows are given in Figs. 4(f), S6(a-b), which are symmetrical triangular, attributed towards good reversibility. This contrast was noted at various potential windows 0–1.4, 0–1.0 and 0–0.8 V at different current densities. The three potential windows with the largest specific capacitance were 300 F/g at 0–0.8 V, 366 F/g at 0–1.2 V and 396 F/g at 0–1.4 V. The relationship between the specific capacitance of MnO2 at different potential windows of 0–1.4, 0–1.0, and 0–0.8 V and current density is displayed in Fig. 4(h). With a potential window of 1.4 V and a current density of 1 A/g, the increased specific capacitance was 396 F/g. The cyclic stability of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs is compared Fig. 4(g) at a current density of 10 A/g over 10,000 cycles in three working potential windows of 0–1.4, 0–1.0, and 0–0.8 V, respectively, to have a thorough understanding of the stability of the K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs electrode at different potential windows. Moreover, the total current contribution comprised of diffusion controlled and surface controlled processes as shown in Fig. 4(i) at various scan rates. Remarkably, the extended potential window has no impact on the K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs electrode's cyclic stability. The power density of anode material was calculated to be 683 W/Kg and energy density is 108 Wh/kg.

The specific capacitance, energy density and power density was calculated by using the following equations
(i)
C p
= I
x
Δt / m
x
ΔV
(ii)
E = 1 / 2
C p
x
Δ
V 2
(iii)
P = E
x
3600 / Δt
where Cp is specific capacitance, I stands for current, m is active mass, Δt is discharge time, ΔV is working potential window, E is energy density and P denotes power density.

Practicability of the as-prepared electrode was evaluated by construction of asymmetric super capacitor with K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs as anode and activated carbon as a cathode in 1–2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solutions as electrolyte. Fig. 5(a) shows the CV of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs//AC at 5, 10, 20, 30, 50 and 75 mVS−1 at a potential window of 0 to 2.4 V. The stable working potential window of the K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs electrode is 0–1.4 V, whereas the AC shows a steady working potential window of 0 to 2.4 V. As a result, an estimate of 2.4 V was made for the asymmetric super capacitor cell voltage for the K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs//AC device. The highest feasible potential window may attain 0–2.4 V prior to the onset of polarization at 1.9 V. Consequently, it was determined that the most practical potential window for ASC, using K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs as the positive electrode, was 1.4 V. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of the K-MnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs is shown in Fig. 5(b). Rs is the first intersection in Nyquist plot with the real axis in high frequency region is attributed to the Ohmic resistance of the internal impedance of the electrode material. The calculated value of solution resistance (Rs) is 6849 Ω between electrode-electrolyte interfaces is responsible for the semicircle. Capacitance characteristics are depicted by the straight line in low frequency range. Higher slope of the straight line and smaller diameter of the semicircle represents faster electronic conductivity and ion diffusion of the K-⸹MnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs. The elevated specific capacitance of 103 F/g Fig. 5(c) was noted for the K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs//AC device at a current density of 0.5 A/g. Fig. 5(d) shows the cycling profile with 98 % capacitance retention over 10,000 cycles at a current density of 10 A/g with voltage windows of 2.4 V Fig. 5(e) shows the GCD curves of K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs//AC device at various current densities of 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 and 2.5 A/g. The GCD curves are nearly symmetrical triangular shaped, showing good reversibility of the device [57,58].

The CCDs were prepared according the reported literature. Casein was obtained by adding acetic acid drop wise to the boiling milk which resulted into curd precipitate in few seconds. The precipitate was filtered and dried in oven at 100 °C for 12 h. 0.1 g. of casein in 20 mL of ethylene glycol and 0.2 g of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) in 20 mL of ethylene glycol solutions were prepared in separate beakers. The two solutions were mixed together and the resultant solution was subjected to microwave irradiations for 30 min at 600 W power. The obtained solution was filtered using 0.2 μm membrane and dialyzed against water using dialysis bag 3.5 kD for two days to remove excess of PVP. The resulting solution was freeze dried and 40 mg of the product is obtained for further fabrication.

K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs nanocomposite was prepared by modified in situ hydrothermal method. Accordingly, (0.2 mmol), K2SO4 (0.2 mmol) and KMnO4 (0.2 mmol) were mixed together in a round bottom flask in 50 mL distilled water. The solution was stirred for 0.5 h. at ambient temperature. The solution was then transferred into 100 mL Teflon-autoclave which have already been charged with 20 mL of CCDs solution and 0.5 g of MWCNTs in distilled water. The autoclave was placed in muffle furnace at 140 °C for 40 min. The autoclave is allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. Then the mixture was subjected to filtration and the final product was washed three times with deionized water and ethanol. Finally, the sample was dried at 80 °C in oven for 12 h [4].

In summary, we have fabricated K intercalated MnO2, coupled with Casein quantum dots decorated carbon nanotubes electrode material for exceptional high energy and power density with extended workable potential window of 0–1.4 V. The specific capacitance was recorded to be 396 F/g which is exceptionally high due to prolong potential window. Asymmetric super capacitor (K-δMnO2/CCDs-MWCNTs//Ac) has been configured with 2.4 V potential window. This device demonstrated a high energy density of 82 Wh/kg and power density of 595 W/kg with excellent cycling stability. The strategy was to fabricate MnO2 based electrode with high ion/charge conductivity, high mechanical stability and extend the potential window for the boosting of energy and power densities to step forward towards practicability of super capacitors applications in energy sectors.

Muhammad Shahab: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Israr Ahmad: Software, Methodology. Noreen Nazeef: Formal analysis, Data curation. Fazal Raziq: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Saharfat Ali: Validation, Formal analysis. Behzad Khan: Resources, Investigation. Majid Khan: Writing – review & editing. Anis Ur Rahman: Formal analysis, Data curation. Khair Zaman: Conceptualization. Muhammad Ibrahim: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Iltaf Muhammad: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Ihsan Ullah: Formal analysis, Data curation. Muhammad Ikram: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Asad Ali: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by International Excellent Young Scientists, National Science Foundation China.
This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China award number 22071102 and 91956110.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2024.112879.
